coredata.dc:title: Novel nanotubes based on methylene-bridged cycloparaphenyleneas as highly efficient catalysts for oxygen evolution reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Abdelsalam, Hazem
Abd-Elkader, Omar H.
Saad, Mohamed A.
Sakr, Mahmoud A.S.
Zhang, Qinfang
coredata.dc:description: Nanotubes constructed from methylene-bridged cycloparaphenyleneas (MCPPs) are investigated using density functional theory. The dynamical stability is indicated by the real vibrational frequencies of the infrared spectra. MCPPs are semiconductors with an energy gap of ∼ 2.9 eV that significantly decreases in the finite nanotubes. This decrease is a result of the interactive molecular orbitals that are localized at the pentagonal rings linking MCPPs. The UV–vis spectra show that transitions from the highest occupied and neighbor orbitals to the lowest unoccupied orbital dominate the primary absorption peaks. Oxygen evolution reaction shows that all the reaction intermediates, HO, O, and HOO are adsorbed by the pentagonal active sites. The considered nanotubes show good catalytic performance but the best performance is observed in the wider nanotubes with an overpotential of 0.10 V. This remarkably low overpotential in addition to the abundant active sites makes finite nanotubes from MCPPs exceptional catalysts for oxygen evolution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methylene-bridged cycloparaphenyleneas
Finite nanotubes
DFT
Electronic and optical properties
Oxygen evolution reaction

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361), and College Natural Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The optimized structure of MCPPs and their based finite nanotubes.
The electronic density of states of [6] MCPP and the corresponding nanotubes (a-c). The same for [8]MCPP is shown in (d-f) and for [10] MCPP is shown in (g-i). The insets represent the HOMO and LUMO.
The UV–Vis absorption spectra of [6] MCPP, [8] MCPP, [10] MCPP and its based finite nanotubes.

(a) the selected sites for the interaction of the [10] MCPP-T2 with the OER intermediates. (b) the non-optimized structure showing the HO intermediate situated at 3 Å above site b. (c-i) the optimized structures of [10] MCPP-T2 adsorbing the intermediates at different sites.
Free energy change diagrams of (a) [10] MCPP-T2, (b) [10] MCPP, (c) [10] MCPP-T3, and (d) [8] MCPP-T2.
The CC bond length (dcc), the binding energy (Eb), bond, and dihedral angles (degrees) of the MCPPs.

The calculated excited state (ES), maximum wavelength (λmax), transition energy (TE), electronic transition (ET), oscillator strength (f), and transition coefficient (TC) for [6] MCPP, [8] MCPP, [10] MCPP and their based nanotubes.
The calculated free energy change for the four OER reaction steps at selected adsorption sites on [10] MCPP-T2, [10] MCPP-T3, and [10] MCPP.

head.ce:title: Novel nanotubes based on methylene-bridged cycloparaphenyleneas as highly efficient catalysts for oxygen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Theoretical Physics Department, National Research Centre, El-Buhouth Str., 12622, Dokki, Giza, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Theoretical Physics Department, National Research Centre, El-Buhouth Str., 12622, Dokki, Giza, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Astronomy, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2455, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Astronomy, College of Science, King Saud University, P.O. Box 2455, Riyadh 11451, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Basic Science (CBS), Misr University for Science and Technology (MUST), 6th October City, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Misr University for Science and Technology (MUST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Basic Science (CBS), Misr University for Science and Technology (MUST), 6th October City, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanotubes constructed from methylene-bridged cycloparaphenyleneas (MCPPs) are investigated using density functional theory. The dynamical stability is indicated by the real vibrational frequencies of the infrared spectra. MCPPs are semiconductors with an energy gap of ∼ 2.9 eV that significantly decreases in the finite nanotubes. This decrease is a result of the interactive molecular orbitals that are localized at the pentagonal rings linking MCPPs. The UV–vis spectra show that transitions from the highest occupied and neighbor orbitals to the lowest unoccupied orbital dominate the primary absorption peaks. Oxygen evolution reaction shows that all the reaction intermediates, HO, O, and HOO are adsorbed by the pentagonal active sites. The considered nanotubes show good catalytic performance but the best performance is observed in the wider nanotubes with an overpotential of 0.10 V. This remarkably low overpotential in addition to the abundant active sites makes finite nanotubes from MCPPs exceptional catalysts for oxygen evolution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic nanocarbons such as carbon nanorings and nanobelts have recently gained significant attention due to their unique properties originating from the highly strained cyclic structure, high symmetry, and cyclic π-systems [1–5]). As a result, they show promising applications in different fields, including electronics [6,7], sensors [8], asymmetric catalysis [9], biological applications [10], environmental applications [11], and other applications [12]. Carbon nanorings are ring-shaped aromatic hydrocarbons that consist of n-units of paraphenes. An important example of carbon nanorings is the [n]Cycloparaphenylene ([n]CPP), n is the number of benzene rings connected in their para-positions, that has been synthesized and investigated in the past decade [13–16]. Following the first synthesis of CPP in 2008 [13], several CPPs have been synthesized and their electronic and optical properties have been unveiled [17–19]. CPPs is an interesting and exceptional molecular structures with unique size dependent cyclic and curved conjugation, energy gap, topological, photophysical properties, and host–guest chemistry [20–24]. The interest in CCPs is not only by these unique properties but also because they are promising building blocks to synthesis structurally uniform armchair carbon nanotubes [25–27].

Carbon nanobelts (CNBs) are another interesting form of cyclic nanocarbons with a more rigid cyclic structure and higher strain energy compared to CCPs. Hydrocarbon nanobelts which are double-stranded and belt-shaped molecules made up of fully unsaturated and fused benzenoid rings have fascinated scientists not only because of their aesthetically appealing structures but also their potential applications in the field of carbon nanotechnology [2,28]. It can also be used as a well-defined template for bottom-up building of uniform single-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [29]. One of the main challenges in synthesizing the nanobelts is the building-up of the energy in the strained structures. The first experimental synthesis of fully unsaturated armchair CNBs was achieved in 2017 by Itami group [30]. Two years later, Miao group announced the synthesis of chiral CNBs [31]. After the first synthesis of armchair CNBs, several CNB derivatives have been sensitized [32–35]. Recently, Segawa et al. and Fan et al. reported the synthesis of twisted Mobius carbon nanobelts with the experimentally confirmed topological chirality that paved the way toward the development of topological carbon nanostructures [36,37].

inspired by the recently synthesized methylene-bridged CPPs(MCPPs) [38], here we extend the MCPPs to finite nanotubes and investigate the effect of quantum size [39] on their electronic, optical, and catalytic properties. Connecting benzene rings through methylene makes MCPP different from CPPs. For instance, the optical properties of MCPPs are different from those of CPPs due to the destabilized lowest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) by the fixation of phenylene rings by the methylene groups. On the other hand, MCPPs have belt-form characteristics induced by methylene bridging. For example, the MCPP reported in ref. [38] is identical to the nanobelts, indenofluorene (IF)-CNBs, proposed and investigated theoretically by Nakano’s group [40,41]. They indicated that IF-CNBs are open-shell systems with multiradical characters that significantly boost the third-order nonlinear optical properties. In this work, we focus on MCPP-based nanotubes (MCPP-NT) and their application as catalysts for oxygen evolution reaction (OER).

The optimized structures and electronic properties of the selected carbon nanorings and their based finite nanotubes are investigated using DFT calculations as implemented in Gaussian 16 [42]. The calculations are performed using the Becke-3-parameter-Lee-Yang-Parr (B3LYP) functional [43,44]. The Grimme’s dispersion correction (gd3) is added to the B3LYP functional to take into account the Van der Waals interactions [45]. The hybrid B3LYP is considered because of its accurate representation of the electronic structure of C-based nanomaterials [38,46–48]. We utilize the 6–31g basis set [49,50] that was tested and shows adequate for C-based systems when considering both the required computational power and the accuracy of the results [51,52]. Moreover, we perform addition calculations on selected MCPPs and nanotubes at a higher level of theory to confirm the accuracy of the current results. Namely, the calculations were made using the basis set 6-311g. The calculations performed for [6]MCPP, [6]MCPP-NT2, and [6]MCPP-NT3 are shown in Fig. 1 (a-c). By comparing the results, we found that energy gaps using the 6-311g basis sets are: 1.73, 1.31, and1.26 eV, respectively, which are almost the same as our results (see Fig. 2 (a-c)) using the moderate 6-31g but with lower computational power in the latter case. The time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations are performed for the first 20 excited states to obtain the optical absorption spectra.

The optimized structures of the three MCPPs that were synthesized recently by Kono et al. in ref. [38] in addition to the finite nanoribbons based on them are shown in Fig. 1. The number in front of MCPP, name [6] MCPP, [8] MCPP, [12] MCPP, is the number of benzene rings connected in their para-positions. In the cases of finite nanotubes, we added NT2 and NT3 to represent the vertical stacking of two and three MCPPs, respectively, to form the finite nanotube. For instance, the acronym, [6] MCPP-NT2 represents a finite nanoribbon by vertical stacking of two [6] MCPP.

Even though some of the considered nanosystems are already synthesized and their stability is confirmed, we calculated their binding energy (Eb) to compare them with the binding strength of other structures. Also the infrared spectra database for possible confirmation/comparison with the IR spectra of the synthesized nanorings. The binding energy is calculated using the following equation.
(1)
E b
=
(
E t
-
(
N C
E C
+
N H
E H
)
)
/
N t
where
N C
,
N H
, and
N t
are the numbers of C, H, and the total number of atoms, respectively. The corresponding energies are
E C
,
E H
, and
E t
, respectively. The calculated negative values of the binding energy, see Table 1 , ensure the stable formation of the nanorings and their based nanotubes. It is noted that the binding energy of the finite nanoribbons has higher binding energy than that of the nanorings. It is even higher than that of the planer hBN [51] or graphene [53] nanoflakes which means higher robustness of the constructed nanotubes. The CC bond length given in Table 1 ranges from 1.39 to 1.53 Å where the shorter bond length is observed between C-atoms in the hexagonal benzene rings while the longer bond length is observed in CC bonds at the methylene bridge. All examined MCPPs and their derivatives exhibit both inner and outer flat surfaces, as evidenced by the C6-C7-C8-C9 dihedral angles ranging from 171.180 to 176.321 degrees. It is noted from Table 1 that the bond angles C1-C2-C3 and C6-C7-C10 indicate sp [2] hybridization. The dihedral angles C2-C3-C4-C5 and C6-C7-C8-C4, ranging from 5.053 to 13.450, demonstrate that the hexagonal rings are not in the same plane as the pentagonal rings in all studied MCPPs and their related materials.

Another important tool used to confirm the dynamical stability of a certain structure is to calculate its vibrational frequency and see if they are real or imaginary. If they are real, then their square is positive which indicates a positive local curvature on the potential energy surface (PES), and thus the energy rises as the atoms vibrate. While a negative square, originating from imaginary frequency, implies negative local curvature and a decrease of the energy as the atoms are displaced. For the current cases of finite nanosystems, the vibrational frequencies can be obtained from the infrared (IR) spectrum. The IR spectra for all the studied nanocarbons are shown in Figure S1 in Appendix A. The positive frequencies and consequently their positive square indicate that the optimized structures correspond to a local minimum on the PES that ensures dynamical stability.

To provide an accurate description of the electronic and optical properties of the considered nanosystems, we need first to know their magnetic state. In order to identify the magnetic state, we performed spin unpolarized (closed-shell) and spin-polarized calculations and compared the ground state energies. It is worth noting that the spin unrestricted calculations give the same value of the ground state energy as the restricted ones which means that the electronic structure is a linear combination of pairs of state functions of opposite spin polarizations but equal weights. Therefore, the considered cyclic nanocarbons with intermediate multiradical character can be well described by the spin-restricted DFT calculations [54].

The electronic properties are investigated here by plotting the total density of states and the highest occupied/lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO/LUMO), see Fig. 2. In addition, the values of the calculated energy gaps are provided in Fig. 2. Starting by [6] MCPP, its HOMO-LUMO energy gap decreases by increasing the length from a single ring to longer nanotubes, namely from 2.77 eV to 1.31 eV in [6] MCPP-T2 and 1.25 eV [6] MCPP-T3. This significant decrease is not only a result of the quantum size effect but also due to the interactive π-electrons from C-atoms of the methylene bridge, that were passivated by two H-atoms in the case of [6] MCPP. These interactive electrons appear in the HOMO and LUMO as the localized cubs on C-atoms of the pentagonal rings, see the insets of Fig. 2 (b). The number of these interactive electrons increases in [6] MCPP-T3 leading to a higher localized distribution of HOMO and LUMO and a narrower energy gap. These interactive electrons are expected to play a crucial role in boosting the catalytic performance as will be discussed in the following section. The same behavior of the energy gap and HOMO/LUMO distributions are observed in [8] MCPP (Fig. 2 d-f) and [10] MCPP (Fig. 2 g-i). Another interesting feature is the peculiar increase of the energy gap by increasing the width from [6]MCPP to [10] MCPP which contradicts the behavior in linear conjugated molecules. This behavior is found also in CPPs and is a result of the interplay between strain and geometry. Strain energy increases as the CPPs become smaller leading to a smaller torsional angle to compensate for the increased strain. The decrease of these angles increases π-overlap between neighboring rings. This increased ring conjugation could be the reason for the lowering of the HOMO-LUMO gap as the cyclic nanocarbons become smaller [16]. On the other hand, it is noted that the energy gap decreases in the case of [10] MCPP structures by increasing the width. This effect may be explained in terms of the competing effect between quantum confinement that tends to decrease the energy gap by increasing size and the effect of wrapping. In the case of [10] MCPP, the effect of quantum effect is higher than that of wrapping, consequently, the energy gap decreases in this case.

In this section, we conducted a detailed examination of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of MCPPs and the corresponding finite nanoribbons. The graphical representation of these spectra can be found in Fig. 3 , and the important data is summarized in Table 2 . Our primary objective was to investigate how changing the diameter and length of MCPP influences their UV–Vis absorption patterns. These patterns provide insights into the electronic properties of the materials and result from transitions between different energy levels of the molecules, known as molecular orbitals (MOs). Consequently, any observable shifts in the absorption peaks are expected to correspond with electronic changes due to alterations in the diameter or length of MCPPs. Our study conclusively revealed that increasing the length of MCPPs had a notable impact on the absorption patterns of the MCPP compounds. Particularly, there is a distinct shift towards longer wavelengths, which is referred to as a “redshift,” as evident in Fig. 3. For instance, when comparing the absorption spectrum of [8] MCPP with [8] MCPP-T2 and [8] MCPP-T3, a noticeable redshift in the main absorption peaks is observed. These absorption peaks are attributed to specific transitions, the main contribution comes from H-2 → L as given in Table 2, and their respective wavelength values were 360.29 nm, 745.64 nm, and 966.71 nm. This redshift indicates a decrease of the optical energy gap by increasing length which agrees with the behavior of the electronic energy gap. On the other hand, when comparing the absorption spectrum of [6] MCPP with [8] MCPP and [10] MCPP, slight redshifts are observed which are consistent with the bathochromic shift observed in the UV–Vis spectra of the experimental samples [38]. These shifts were attributed to specific transitions, labeled as H-2 → L, H-1 → L, and H-2 → L, and their respective wavelength values were 360.29 nm, 382.21 nm, and 333.63 nm. A significant observation was also made in Fig. 3: the absorption spectra of [6] MCPP-NT2 and [6] MCPP-NT3, [8] MCPP-NT2 and [8] MCPP-NT3, [10] MCPP-NT2 and [10] MCPP-NT3 show broader patterns compared to [6] MCPP, [8] MCPP, and [10] MCPP. This broadening effect could be attributed to the increased length of MCPP NTs. As the length increased, the absorption peaks spread out, setting these spectra apart from those of [6] MCPP, [8] MCPP, and [10] MCPP.

The interactive localized states observed in the finite nanotubes based on the MCPPs are expected to be promising for different applications such as catalysis and gas sensing. Here we investigated the catalytic performance of [10] MCPPs, as an example, toward oxygen evolution reaction (OER). The electrocatalytic water splitting in an acidic environment can be divided into two reactions [39]: (a) the oxygen evolution reaction at the anode (eq. (2) and the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode (eq. (3).
(2)
2 H
2
O + ∗ → 4
H
+
+
O 2
+ 4
e
-
(3)
2
H
+
+ 2
e
-
+ ∗ →
H 2
+ ∗
where
∗
is the catalyst. It is worth noting that the HER is also tested here however we found that the change in the free energy is very higher up to 2 eV which means that MCPPs are poor electrocatalysts for HER. Therefore, we focus on the first half of the reaction given in equation (2) which can be divided into the following four steps [55]. The free energy change (ΔG) for each reaction is given below.
(4)
2 H
2
O + ∗ →
H 2
O +
HO
∗
+
H
+
+
e
-
Δ
G 1
= Δ
G
OH
- Δ
G
H 2 O
- e U
(5)
H 2
O +
HO
∗
+
H
+

H 2

∗
+
2 ( H
+

)

= Δ
G O

HO

(6)
H 2

O
∗
+
2 ( H
+

) →
HOO

+

)

- Δ

(7)

∗
+

4 ( H

Δ

= Δ
G
O 2
- Δ

HOO
- e U
HO
∗
,
O
∗
, and
HOO
∗
represent the adsorbed intermediates HO, O, and HOO, respectively by the active sites at the catalyst.
Δ
G 1
,
Δ
G 2
,
Δ
G 3
, and ΔG4 are the change in the free energy of the four reaction steps. The free energy change is calculated using equation (8) [56].
(8)
Δ G = Δ E + Δ Z P E - T Δ S + Δ
G U
+ Δ
G
pH
where
Δ E
is the adsorption energy of the intermediate,
Δ Z P E
is the change in the zero-point energy after adsorption, and ΔS is the change in the entropy.
Δ
G U
= - e U
, with
U
is the potential of the electrode relative to the hydrogen electrode which is considered here to be zero. In this work, the calculations were done at the standard conditions of pH = 0, T = 298 K, and P = 1 thus the term ΔGpH will be ignored. For an ideal catalyst a thermodynamic potential equal to 4.92 V is required for water splitting, i.e. 1.23 V for each reaction step. However, in the real case additional potential called “overpotential” is needed for water splitting to overcome the intrinsic activation barriers of the catalyst itself and other barriers from the interaction medium.[57] Therefore, the overpotential (η) for OER can be calculated by subtraction 1.23 V from the maximum ΔG of the four steps as follows.
(9)
Δ
G
OER
= max
Δ
G 1
, Δ
G 2
, Δ
G 3
, Δ
G 4
(10)
η =
Δ
G
OER
e
- 1.23
The different sites considered for the OER on the [10] MCPP are shown in Fig. 4 (a). These sites are: a- above the center of the hexagonal ring at the edge, b- above the heptagonal ring in the center, and c- above the pentagonal ring at the edge. For all the sites the intermediates are located at 3 Å above the selected site, see Fig. 4 (b) for the initial structure of O-intermediate adsorbed on the b-site. By these selected sites we expect that we cover all possible sites for the adsorption of intermediates because, at this moderate separation, the intermediates are free to interact with C-atoms from hexagonal, heptagonal, or pentagonal rings at the edges or the surface of the [10] MCPP. The optimized structures after the adsorption of the three intermediates (HO, O, HOO) in the a-site are presented in Fig. 4 (c-e). It is observed that the intermediates are adsorbed (with strong chemical bonds in some cases) on C-atoms linking the pentagonal to the hexagonal rings. This indicates that these sites are active sites for OER due to the localized pi-electrons discussed before. It is worth noting that putting the intermediates far from these sites, at site b for instance, gives the same results, and the intermediates are bound to the same C-atoms. As seen in Fig. 4 (f) for the adsorption of HO at the b-site where HO was adsorbed by C-atom from the upper pentagonal ring instead of the lower C-atoms from the hexagonal ring. C-atoms on the lower pentagonal ring are also active sites for the OER as seen by the adsorption of the three intermediates at the c-site in Fig. 4 (g-i). Therefore, we conclude that the pentagonal rings in these MCPPs are the active sites for OER due to the localized π-electrons on their C-atoms.

The calculated change in the free energy for the adsorption of HO, O, and HOO at the three positions is given in Table 3 and shown in Fig. 5 . The maximum change in the free energy for OER at site-a equals 1.332 eV observed in the third reaction step. Using equation (10), the corresponding overpotential is 0.102 V which is remarkably low reflecting exceptional catalytic performance toward OER. Oxygen evolution at site-b has a similar behavior with almost the same overpotential but with the highest free energy change in the first reaction step as seen in Fig. 5 (a). The overpotential becomes higher (η = 0.276 V) at site-c which is a result of the higher energy required for the first reaction step. However, the overpotential in all cases shows that [10]MCPP-T2 is a promising catalyst for OER. We then compare this catalytic performance with [10]MCPP and [8]MCPP-T3. For [10]MCPP, the overpotential is considerably high with η = 0.49 and 0.83 V for site-a and site-b, respectively. The maximum change in the free energy is observed at the first reaction step, see Table 3, which means that the high value of η is a result of the low adsorption of HO intermediate by the catalyst. This low adsorption in [10]MCPP compared to [10]MCPP-T2 could be a result of the 2H passivating atoms in the methylene ground that decrease the number of the interactive π-electrons in the former. For the third case of [10]MCPP-T3, we tested only the interaction at site-a but on the surface, namely above C-atoms linking the pentagonal rings to other rings because the interactive π-electrons are localized there. The maximum free energy change is observed in the third reaction step, as seen in Fig. 5 (c), which is similar to that in [10]MCPP-T2. The overpotential is equal to 0.39 V which indicates that [10]MCPP-T3 is also a potential catalyst for OER. The effect of the cyclic geometry on the OER is investigated by comparing the catalytic performance of [10]MCPP-T2 with that of [6]MCPP-T2 and [8]MCPP-T2. According to the values given in Table 3 for the free energy change, the highest change is observed in the third reaction step for both cases, see Fig. 5 (d) also. The corresponding overpotential values are 0.31 V and 0.41 V for [6]MCPP-T2 and [8]MCPP-T2, respectively. This means that the MCPPs-based nanotubes with smaller diameters are not as efficient as the wider [10]MCPP-T2 for OER catalysis.

The electronic, optical, and OER catalytic performance of methylene-bridged cycloparaphenyleneas (MCPPs) and their finite nanotubes are investigated using DFT calculations. The structures are geometrically stable as confirmed by the positive binding energy and the real vibrational frequencies obtained from the infrared spectra. MCPPs are semiconductor nanorings with an energy gap of ∼ 2.8 eV that sharply decreases by increasing the length to form finite nanoribbons. This decrease is a result of the evolution of localized molecular orbital with interactive electrons as observed from the change from the delocalized to the localized distribution of the low energy molecular orbitals in MCPPs and the finite nanotubes, respectively. These interactive electrons which come from C-atoms at the pentagonal rings are promising active sites that boost the catalytic activity of the considered nanocarbons. The UV–Vis absorption spectra show also a decrease in the optical energy gap by moving from MCPPs to finite nanotubes with higher lengths. The optical spectra indicate that the main absorption peaks are dominated by transitions from the highest occupied and neighbor molecular orbital to the lowest unoccupied orbital. The OER is considered on different sites to identify the active sites. The main adsorption sites in all the cases are the active sites at the pentagonal rings that form moderate bonds with the reaction intermediates, namely HO, O, and HOO. The catalytic performance toward OER was calculated for different structures, the one that shows the best performance is the wider finite nanotubes with an overpotential of 0.10 V. This extremely low overpotential and the abundant active sites render the finite nanotubes constructed from MCPPs exceptional catalysts for oxygen evolution.
Data availability: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

Hazem Abdelsalam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Omar H. Abd-Elkader: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mohamed A. Saad: Visualization, Software, Investigation, Conceptualization. Mahmoud A.S. Sakr: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Qinfang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361), and College Natural Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The infrared spectra with positive frequencies for the investigated MCPPs and their nanotubes are shown in Figure S1. The plots show that the frequencies are real, therefore, their square is positive and the optimized structures correspond to a local minimum on the potential energy surface of the B3LYP functional. Which means that the optimized structures are dynamically stable.
Fig. S1
The infrared spectra of MCPPs and their based finite nanotubes.
